sentences.id: We are still at the beginning of this revolution and small choices now may turn out to have gigantic consequences later. (21 words)
sentences.en: 我们还在这场革命的起始阶段，现在小小的选择或许在今后产生大大的影响。
sentences.cn: A long struggle will be needed to avoid a future of digital feudalism. (13 words)
sentences.words: 为避免未来出现数字封建主义，需要付出长期的努力。

sentences.id: Ms Denham’s report is a welcome start. (7 words)
sentences.en: 德纳姆女士的报告是一个良好的开端。
sentences.cn:
1804
The U.S. Postal Service (USPS) continues to bleed red ink. (10 words)
sentences.words: 美国邮政署（USPS）还在亏钱。

sentences.id: It reported a net loss of $5.6 billion for fiscal 2016, the 10th straight year its expenses have exceeded revenue. (20 words)
sentences.en: 2016财年，其公布的净亏损额高达56亿美元，已经连续10年入不敷出了。
sentences.cn: Meanwhile, it has more than $120 billion in unfunded liabilities, mostly for employee health and retirement costs. (17 words)
sentences.words: 同时，它还有超过1200亿美元的无资金准备债务，主要是员工的医疗费和退休金。

sentences.id: There are many reasons this formerly stable federal institution finds itself on the verge of bankruptcy. (16 words)
sentences.en: Fundamentally, the USPS is in a historic squeeze between technological change that has permanently decreased demand for its bread-and-butter product, first-class mail, and a regulatory structure that denies management the flexibility to adjust its operations to the new reality. (39 words)
sentences.cn: 从根本上说，美国邮政署处于一种历史的夹缝之中：一边是技术革新使其核心业务，普通邮件的需求量下滑，永无回升之力；一边是监管架构使其管理无法变通，不能针对新的现实来调整其运营方式。
sentences.words: And interest groups ranging from postal unions to greeting-card makers exert self-interested pressure on the USPS’s ultimate overseer—Congress—insisting that whatever else happens to the Postal Service, aspects of the status quo they depend on get protected. (38 words)

sentences.id: 从邮政工会到贺卡制造商的各个利益集团处于自己的私立，都在给美国邮政的最终监管机构——国会施压，他们坚持要求，不管邮政署其他方面发生了什么，他们利益所在的方方面面的现状都要得到保护。
sentences.en: This is why repeated attempts at reform legislation have failed in recent years, leaving the Postal Service unable to pay its bills except by deferring vital modernization. (27 words)
sentences.cn: 这也解释了为什么最近几年一再努力，改革立法，均以失败告终，只留下邮政署无力支付账单，不得不推迟其至关重要的现代化改革。
sentences.words: Now comes word that everyone involved—Democrats, Republicans, the Postal Service, the unions and the system’s heaviest users—has finally agreed on a plan to fix the system. (28 words)

sentences.id: 现在传来的消息是，有关的各方，包括民主党、共和党、美国邮政、工会组织，还有系统的极重度用户，最终都已经达成计划，解决邮政系统的问题。
sentences.en: Legislation is moving through the House that would save USPS an estimated $28.6 billion over five years, which could help pay for new vehicles, among other survival measures. (28 words)
sentences.cn: 众议院正要进行立法，这项法案预估能使美国邮政署在五年内节省大约286亿美元，这些钱能帮助他们购买新的运输工具，还可以花在其他维持生存的措施上。
sentences.words: Most of the money would come from a penny-per-letter permanent rate increase and from shifting postal retirees into Medicare. (19 words)

sentences.id: 这笔钱主要来自两个方面，一是把每封信的固定邮费上调一美分，二是把邮政退休员工转到医保系统。
sentences.en: The latter step would largely offset the financial burden of annually pre-funding retiree health care, thus addressing a long-standing complaint by the USPS and its unions. (26 words)
sentences.cn: 后一项举措将会大大抵消每年为退休人员预付医保而带来的财务负担，这样也解决了美国邮政署及其公会长期抱怨的问题。
sentences.words: If it clears the House, this measure would still have to get through the Senate—where someone is bound to point out that it amounts to the bare, bare minimum necessary to keep the Postal Service afloat, not comprehensive reform. (40 words)

sentences.id: 如果这一措施在众议院通过，它还得参议院通过——在参议院肯定有人会说，这只是能让邮政署勉强维持下去的最低，最低的救助方案，而不是全面的改革。
sentences.en: There’s no change to collective bargaining at the USPS, a major omission considering that personnel accounts for 80 percent of the agency’s costs. (23 words)
sentences.cn: 美国邮政署内的集体谈判没有任何变化，考虑到员工支出占到公司成本的80%，这是一个重大遗漏。
sentences.words: Also missing is any discussion of eliminating Saturday letter delivery. (10 words)

sentences.id: 也根本没有讨论是否要取消周六信件投递的问题。
sentences.en: That common-sense change enjoys wide public support and would save the USPS $2 billion per year. (16 words)
sentences.cn: 这种按照常理做出的改变，得到大众广泛的支持，并且将为美国邮政署每年节省20亿美元。
sentences.words: But postal special-interest groups seem to have killed it, at least in the House. (14 words)

sentences.id: 但是邮政系统的特殊利益集团似乎已经将其扼杀，至少在众议院是如此。
sentences.en: The emerging consensus around the bill is a sign that legislators are getting frightened about a politically embarrassing short-term collapse at the USPS. (23 words)
sentences.cn: 围绕这一法案达成的初步共识表明，立法人员担心的是美国邮政署短期的崩溃会给他们带来政治上的难堪。
sentences.words: It is not, however, a sign that they’re getting serious about transforming the postal system for the 21st century. (19 words)

sentences.id: 然而，这也表明，他们并没有认真对待邮政系统面向21世纪的转型问题。
sentences.en:
1901
Financial regulators in Britain have imposed a rather unusual rule on the bosses of big banks. (16 words)
sentences.cn: 英国金融监管机构给几大银行的老总强制推行了一项极不寻常的规定。
sentences.words: Starting next year, any guaranteed bonus of top executives could be delayed 10 years if their banks are under investigation for wrongdoing. (22 words)

sentences.id: 明年起，如果银行因不当行为受到调查，其高管的所有固定将近都可能会推迟10年发放。
sentences.en: The main purpose of this “clawback” rule is to hold bankers accountable for harmful risk-taking and to restore public trust in financial institutions. (23 words)
sentences.cn: 这项“追款”规定的主要目的是要让银行家为有害的冒险行为负责，重塑公众对金融机构的信任。
sentences.words: Yet officials also hope for a much larger benefit: more long-term decision-making, not only by banks but by all corporations, to build a stronger economy for future generations. (28 words)

sentences.id: 然而，官员们还期望得到更大的利益：无论是银行还是所有公司，都要做更长远的决策，为子孙后代打造更强的经济。
sentences.en: “Short-termism” or the desire for quick profits, has worsened in publicly traded companies, says the Bank of England’s top economist, Andrew Haldane. (22 words)
sentences.cn: “短期主义”，或者说渴望快速获利，在上市公司中愈演愈烈，英格兰银行首席经济学家安德鲁·霍尔丹这样说道。
sentences.words: He quotes a giant of classical economics, Alfred Marshall, in describing this financial impatience as acting like “children who pick the plums out of their pudding to eat them at once” rather than putting them aside to be eaten last. (40 words)

sentences.id: 他援引古典经济学巨匠阿尔弗雷德·马歇尔的话，把这种金融上的急躁行为形容成是“孩子从布丁里挑出葡萄干马上就吃了”，而不是放在一边最后吃。
sentences.en: The average time for holding a stock in both the United States and Britain, he notes, has dropped from seven years to seven months in recent decades. (27 words)
sentences.cn: 在英美两国，持有一只股票的平均时长，他指出，在最近几十年从七年减少至七个月。
sentences.words: Transient investors, who demand high quarterly profits from companies, can hinder a firm’s efforts to invest in long-term research or to build up customer loyalty. (25 words)

sentences.id: 短期投资者要求每个季度都能从公司获得高额利润，而公司要尽力投资长期的研发，要尽力增强客户的忠实度，他们可能会成为阻碍。
sentences.en: This has been dubbed “quarterly capitalism”. (6 words)
sentences.cn: 这就是人们所称的“季度资本主义”。
sentences.words: In addition, new digital technologies have allowed more rapid trading of equities, quicker use of information, and thus shorter attention spans in financial markets. (24 words)

sentences.id:
此外，新的数字技术使得股票交易更快速，信息使用更高效，因此使得金融
市场更加急功近利。
sentences.en: “There seems to be a predominance of short-term thinking at the expense of long-term investing,” said Commissioner Daniel Gallagher of the US Securities and Exchange Commission in a speech this week. (31 words)
sentences.cn: “占主导地位的似乎是短线思维，其代价是损害长期投资”，美国证券交易委员会委员丹尼尔·盖伦格在本周的一场演讲中这样说道。
sentences.words: In the US, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 has pushed most public companies to defer performance bonuses for senior executives by about a year, slightly helping reduce “short-termism.” (28 words)

sentences.id: 在美国，已经迫使大多数上市公司推迟一年左右发放高管的绩效津贴，略微有助于减少“短期主义”现象。
sentences.en: In its latest survey of CEO pay, The Wall Street Journal finds that “a substantial part” of executive pay is now tied to performance. (24 words)
sentences.cn: 《华尔街日报》最近调查了首席执行官的薪酬，发现高管薪酬的“大部分”现在都与业绩挂钩。
sentences.words: Much more could be done to encourage “long-termism,” such as changes in the tax code and quicker disclosure of stock acquisitions. (21 words)

sentences.id: 要鼓励“长期主义”，我们要做的还有很多，比如修订税法，加快披露股票收购。
sentences.en: In France, shareholders who hold onto a company investment for at least two years can sometimes earn more voting rights in a company. (23 words)
sentences.cn: 在法国，持有某个公司股票两年以上的股东有时候在公司会有更大的投票权。
sentences.words: Within companies, the right compensation design can provide incentives for executives to think beyond their own time at the company and on behalf of all stakeholders. (26 words)

sentences.id: 在公司内部，合理的薪酬设计能激励管理者，让他们能够深谋远虑，不局限与自己在公司的任期期限，还能为所有股东着想。
sentences.en: Britain’s new rule is a reminder to bankers that society has an interest in their performance, not just for the short term but for the long term. (27 words)
sentences.cn: 英国的这条新政是在提醒银行家，社会在关注他们的业绩，不只是短期业绩，还有长期业绩。
sentences.words:
1902
Grade inflation—the gradual increase in average GPAs (grade-point averages) over the past few decades—is often considered a product of a consumer era in higher education, in which students are treated like customers to be pleased. (37 words)

sentences.id: 成绩通胀——近几十年学生的平均绩点（GPA）逐渐上升——常常看成是高等教育消费者时代的产物；在这样的时代下，学生被当做客户，是需要取悦的对象。
sentences.en: But another, related force—a policy often buried deep in course catalogs called “grade forgiveness”—is helping raise GPAs. (19 words)
sentences.cn: 但另一个相关因素——通常深藏在课程目录里叫做“成绩宽容”的政策——也在助长绩点的走高。
sentences.words: Grade forgiveness allows students to retake a course in which they received a low grade, and the most recent grade or the highest grade is the only one that counts in calculating a student’s overall GPA. (36 words)

sentences.id: 成绩宽容政策可以让学生重修得分较低的课程，并且在计算某个学生总绩点的时候只统计最近的或者最高的分数。
sentences.en: The use of this little-known practice has accelerated in recent years, as colleges continue to do their utmost to keep students in school (and paying tuition) and improve their graduation rates. (31 words)
sentences.cn: 近年来，这一鲜为人知的做法得以加速应用，因为各大高校还在竭尽全力留住学生（收学费），提高毕业率。
sentences.words: When this practice first started decades ago, it was usually limited to freshmen, to give them a second chance to take a class in their first year if they struggled in their transition to college-level courses. (36 words)

sentences.id: But now most colleges, save for many selective campuses, allow all undergraduates, and even graduate students, to get their low grades forgiven. (22 words)
sentences.en: College officials tend to emphasize that the goal of grade forgiveness is less about the grade itself and more about encouraging students to retake courses critical to their degree program and graduation without incurring a big penalty. (37 words)
sentences.cn: “Ultimately,” said Jack Miner, Ohio State University’s registrar, “we see students achieve more success because they retake a course and do better in subsequent courses or master the content that allows them to graduate on time.” (36 words)
sentences.words: That said, there is a way in which grade forgiveness satisfies colleges’ own needs as well. (16 words)

sentences.id: For public institutions, state funds are sometimes tied partly to their success on metrics such as graduation rates and student retention—so better grades can, by boosting figures like those, mean more money. (33 words)
sentences.en: And anything that raises GPAs will likely make students—who, at the end of the day, are paying the bill—feel they’ve gotten a better value for their tuition dollars, which is another big concern for colleges. (37 words)
sentences.cn: Indeed, grade forgiveness is just another way that universities are responding to consumers’ expectations for higher education. (17 words)
sentences.words: Since students and parents expect a college degree to lead to a job, it is in the best interest of a school to turn out graduates who are as qualified as possible—or at least appear to be. (38 words)

sentences.id: On this, students’ and colleges’ incentives seem to be aligned. (10 words)
sentences.en:
1903
This year marks exactly two centuries since the publication of Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus, by Mary Shelley. (18 words)
sentences.cn: Even before the invention of the electric light bulb, the author produced a remarkable work of speculative fiction that would foreshadow many ethical questions to be raised by technologies yet to come. (32 words)
sentences.words: Today the rapid growth of artificial intelligence (AI) raises fundamental questions: “What is intelligence, identity, or consciousness? (17 words)

sentences.id: What makes humans humans?”(4 words)
sentences.en: What is being called artificial general intelligence, machines that would imitate the way humans think, continues to evade scientists. (19 words)
sentences.cn:
Yet humans remain fascinated by the idea of robots that would look, move, and respond like humans, similar to those recently depicted on popular sci-fi TV series such as “Westworld” and “Humans.”(32 words)
Just how people think is still far too complex to be understood, let alone reproduced, says David Eagleman, a Stanford University neuroscientist. (22 words)
sentences.words: “We are just in a situation where there are no good theories explaining what consciousness actually is and how you could ever build a machine to get there.” (28 words)

sentences.id: But that doesn’t mean crucial ethical issues involving AI aren’t at hand. (12 words)
sentences.en: The coming use of autonomous vehicles, for example, poses thorny ethical questions. (12 words)
sentences.cn: Human drivers sometimes must make split-second decisions. (7 words)
sentences.words: Their reactions may be a complex combination of instant reflexes, input from past driving experiences, and what their eyes and ears tell them in that moment. (26 words)

sentences.id: AI “vision” today is not nearly as sophisticated as that of humans. (12 words)
sentences.en: And to anticipate every imaginable driving situation is a difficult programming problem. (12 words)
sentences.cn: Whenever decisions are based on masses of data, “you quickly get into a lot of ethical questions,” notes Tan Kiat How, chief executive of a Singapore-based agency that is helping the government develop a voluntary code for the ethical use of AI. (42 words)
sentences.words: Along with Singapore, other governments and mega-corporations are beginning to establish their own guidelines. (14 words)

sentences.id: Britain is setting up a data ethics center. (8 words)
sentences.en: India released its AI ethics strategy this spring. (8 words)
sentences.cn: On June 7 Google pledged not to “design or deploy AI” that would cause “overall harm,” or to develop AI-directed weapons or use AI for surveillance that would violate international norms. (31 words)
sentences.words: It also pledged not to deploy AI whose use would violate international laws or human rights. (16 words)

sentences.id: While the statement is vague, it represents one starting point. (10 words)
sentences.en: So does the idea that decisions made by AI systems should be explainable, transparent, and fair. (16 words)
sentences.cn: To put it another way: How can we make sure that the thinking of intelligent machines reflects humanity’s highest values? (20 words)
sentences.words: Only then will they be useful servants and not Frankenstein’s out-of-control monster. (12 words)

sentences.id:
1904
States will be able to force more people to pay sales tax when they make online purchases under a Supreme Court decision Thursday that will leave shoppers with lighter wallets but is a big financial win for states. (38 words)
sentences.en: The Supreme Court’s opinion Thursday overruled a pair of decades-old decisions that states said cost them billions of dollars in lost revenue annually. (23 words)
sentences.cn: The decisions made it more difficult for states to collect sales tax on certain online purchases. (16 words)
sentences.words: The cases the court overturned said that if a business was shipping a customer’s purchase to a state where the business didn’t have a physical presence such as a warehouse or office, the business didn’t have to collect sales tax for the state. (43 words)

sentences.id: Customers were generally responsible for paying the sales tax to the state themselves if they weren’t charged it, but most didn’t realize they owed it and few paid. (28 words)
sentences.en: Justice Anthony Kennedy wrote that the previous decisions were flawed. (10 words)
sentences.cn: “Each year the physical presence rule becomes further removed from economic reality and results in significant revenue losses to the states,” he wrote in an opinion joined by four other justices. (31 words)
sentences.words: Kennedy wrote that the rule “limited states’ ability to seek long-term prosperity and has prevented market participants from competing on an even playing field.” (24 words)

sentences.id: The ruling is a victory for big chains with a presence in many states, since they usually collect sales tax on online purchases already. (24 words)
sentences.en: Now, rivals will be charging sales tax where they hadn’t before. (11 words)
sentences.cn: Big chains have been collecting sales tax nationwide because they typically have physical stores in whatever state a purchase is being shipped to. (23 words)
sentences.words: Amazon.com, with its network of warehouses, also collects sales tax in every state that charges it, though third-party sellers who use the site don’t have to. (26 words)

sentences.id: Until now, many sellers that have a physical presence in only a single state or a few states have been able to avoid charging sales taxes when they ship to addresses outside those states. (34 words)
sentences.en: Sellers that use eBay and Etsy, which provide platforms for smaller sellers, also haven’t been collecting sales tax nationwide. (19 words)
sentences.cn: Under the ruling Thursday, states can pass laws requiring out-of-state sellers to collect the state’s sales tax from customers and send it to the state. (25 words)
sentences.words: Retail trade groups praised the ruling, saying it levels the playing field for local and online businesses. (17 words)

sentences.id: The losers, said retail analyst Neil Saunders, are online-only retailers, especially smaller ones. (13 words)
sentences.en: Those retailers may face headaches complying with various state sales tax laws. (12 words)
sentences.cn:
The Small Business & Entrepreneurship Council advocacy group said in a statement, “Small businesses and internet entrepreneurs are not well served at all by this decision.” (26 words)

2001
A group of labour MPs, among them Yvette Cooper, are bringing in the new year with a call to institute a UK “town of culture” award. (26 words)
sentences.words: The proposal is that it should sit alongside the existing city of culture title, which was held by Hull in 2017, and has been awarded to Coventry for 2021. (29 words)

sentences.id: Cooper and her colleagues argue that the success of the crown for Hull, where it brought in £220m of investment and an avalanche of arts, ought not to be confined to cities. (32 words)
sentences.en: Britain’s towns, it is true, are not prevented from applying, but they generally lack the resources to put together a bid to beat their bigger competitors. (26 words)
sentences.cn: A town of culture award could, it is argued, become an annual event, attracting funding and creating jobs. (18 words)
sentences.words: Some might see the proposal as a booby prize for the fact that Britain is no longer able to apply for the much more prestigious title of European capital of culture, a sought-after award bagged by Glasgow in 1990 and Liverpool in 2008. (43 words)

sentences.id: A cynic might speculate that the UK is on the verge of disappearing into an endless fever of self-celebration in its desperation to reinvent itself for the post-Brexit world: after town of culture, who knows what will follow—village of culture? (41 words)
sentences.en: Suburb of culture? (3 words)
sentences.cn: Hamlet of culture? (3 words)
sentences.words: It is also wise to recall that such titles are not a cure-all. (13 words)

sentences.id: A badly run “year of culture” washes in and out of a place like the tide, bringing prominence for a spell but leaving no lasting benefits to the community. (29 words)
sentences.en: The really successful holders of such titles are those that do a great deal more than fill hotel bedrooms and bring in high-profile arts events and good press for a year. (31 words)
sentences.cn: They transform the aspirations of the people who live there; they nudge the self-image of the city into a bolder and more optimistic light. (24 words)
sentences.words: It is hard to get right, and requires a remarkable degree of vision, as well as cooperation between city authorities, the private sector, community groups and cultural organisations. (28 words)

sentences.id: But it can be done: Glasgow’s year as European capital of culture can certainly be seen as one of a complex series of factors that have turned the city into the powerhouse of art, music and theatre that it remains today. (41 words)
sentences.en: A “town of culture” could be not just about the arts but about honouring a town’s peculiarities—helping sustain its high street, supporting local facilities and above all celebrating its people. (31 words)
sentences.cn: Jeremy Wright, the culture secretary, should welcome this positive, hope-filled proposal, and turn it into action. (16 words)
sentences.words:
2002
Scientific publishing has long been a licence to print money. (10 words)

sentences.id: Scientists need journals in which to publish their research, so they will supply the articles without monetary reward. (18 words)
sentences.en: Other scientists perform the specialised work of peer review also for free, because it is a central element in the acquisition of status and the production of scientific knowledge. (29 words)
sentences.cn: With the content of papers secured for free, the publisher needs only to find a market for its journal. (19 words)
sentences.words: Until this century, university libraries were not very price sensitive. (10 words)

sentences.id: Scientific publishers routinely report profit margins approaching 40% on their operations, at a time when the rest of the publishing industry is in an existential crisis. (26 words)
sentences.en: The Dutch giant Elsevier, which claims to publish 25% of the scientific papers produced in the world, made profits of more than £900m last year, while UK universities alone spent more than £210m in 2016 to enable researchers to access their own publicly funded research; both figures seem to rise unstoppably despite increasingly desperate efforts to change them. (58 words)
sentences.cn: The most drastic, and thoroughly illegal, reaction has been the emergence of Sci-Hub, a kind of global photocopier for scientific papers, set up in 2012, which now claims to offer access to every paywalled article published since 2015. (38 words)
sentences.words: The success of Sci-Hub, which relies on researchers passing on copies they have themselves legally accessed, shows the legal ecosystem has lost legitimacy among its users and must be transformed so that it works for all participants. (37 words)

sentences.id: In Britain the move towards open access publishing has been driven by funding bodies. (14 words)
sentences.en: In some ways it has been very successful. (8 words)
sentences.cn: More than half of all British scientific research is now published under open access terms: either freely available from the moment of publication, or paywalled for a year or more so that the publishers can make a profit before being placed on general release. (44 words)
sentences.words: Yet the new system has not worked out any cheaper for the universities. (13 words)

sentences.id: Publishers have responded to the demand that they make their product free to readers by charging their writers fees to cover the costs of preparing an article. (27 words)
sentences.en: These range from around ￡500 to $5,000. (7 words)
sentences.cn: A report last year pointed out that the costs both of subscriptions and of these “article preparation costs” had been steadily rising at a rate above inflation. (27 words)
sentences.words: In some ways the scientific publishing model resembles the economy of the social Internet: labour is provided free in exchange for the hope of status, while huge profits are made by a few big firms who run the market places. (40 words)

sentences.id: In both cases, we need a rebalancing of power. (9 words)
sentences.en:
2003
Progressives often support diversity mandates as a path to equality and a way to level the playing field. (18 words)
sentences.cn: But all too often such policies are an insincere form of virtue-signaling that benefits only the most privileged and does little to help average people. (25 words)
sentences.words: A pair of bills sponsored by Massachusetts state Senator Jason Lewis and House Speaker Pro Tempore Patricia Haddad, to ensure “gender parity” on boards and commissions, provide a case in point. (31 words)

sentences.id: Haddad and Lewis are concerned that more than half the state-government boards are less than 40 percent female. (18 words)
sentences.en: In order to ensure that elite women have more such opportunities, they have proposed imposing government quotas. (17 words)
sentences.cn: If the bills become law, state boards and commissions will be required to set aside 50 percent of board seats for women by 2022. (24 words)
sentences.words: The bills are similar to a measure recently adopted in California, which last year became the first state to require gender quotas for private companies. (25 words)

sentences.id: In signing the measure, California Governor Jerry Brown admitted that the law, which expressly classifies people on the basis of sex, is probably unconstitutional. (24 words)
sentences.en: The US Supreme Court frowns on sex-based classifications unless they are designed to address an “important” policy interest. (18 words)
sentences.cn: Because the California law applies to all boards, even where there is no history of prior discrimination, courts are likely to rule that the law violates the constitutional guarantee of “equal protection.”(32 words)
sentences.words: But are such government mandates even necessary? (7 words)

sentences.id: Female participation on corporate boards may not currently mirror the percentage of women in the general population, but so what? (20 words)
sentences.en: The number of women on corporate boards has been steadily increasing without government interference. (14 words)
sentences.cn: According to a study by Catalyst, between 2010 and 2015 the share of women on the boards of global corporations increased by 54 percent. (24 words)
sentences.words: Requiring companies to make gender the primary qualification for board membership will inevitably lead to less experienced private sector boards. (20 words)

sentences.id: That is exactly what happened when Norway adopted a nationwide corporate gender quota. (13 words)
sentences.en: Writing in The New Republic, Alice Lee notes that increasing the number of opportunities for board membership without increasing the pool of qualified women to serve on such boards has led to a “golden skirt” phenomenon, where the same elite women scoop up multiple seats on a variety of boards. (50 words)
sentences.cn: Next time somebody pushes corporate quotas as a way to promote gender equity, remember that such policies are largely self-serving measures that make their sponsors feel good but do little to help average women. (34 words)
sentences.words:
2004
Last Thursday, the French Senate passed a digital services tax, which would impose an entirely new tax on large multinationals that provide digital services to consumers or users in France. (30 words)

sentences.id: Digital services include everything from providing a platform for selling goods and services online to targeting advertising based on user data, and the tax applies to gross revenue from such services. (31 words)
sentences.en: Many French politicians and media outlets have referred to this as a “GAFA tax,” meaning that it is designed to apply primarily to companies such as Google, Apple, Facebook and Amazon—in other words, multinational tech companies based in the United States. (42 words)
sentences.cn: The digital services tax now awaits the signature of President Emmanuel Macron, who has expressed support for the measure, and it could go into effect within the next few weeks. (30 words)
sentences.words: But it has already sparked significant controversy, with the United States trade representative opening an investigation into whether the tax discriminates against American companies, which in turn could lead to trade sanctions against France. (34 words)

sentences.id: The French tax is not just a unilateral move by one country in need of revenue. (16 words)
sentences.en: Instead, the digital services tax is part of a much larger trend, with countries over the past few years proposing or putting in place an alphabet soup of new international tax provisions. (32 words)
sentences.cn: These have included Britain’s DPT (diverted profits tax), Australia’s MAAL (multinational antiavoidance law), and India’s SEP (significant economic presence) test, to name but a few. (25 words)
sentences.words: At the same time, the European Union, Spain, Britain and several other countries have all seriously contemplated digital services taxes. (20 words)

sentences.id: These unilateral developments differ in their specifics, but they are all designed to tax multinationals on income and revenue that countries believe they should have a right to tax, even if international tax rules do not grant them that right. (40 words)
sentences.en: In other words, they all share a view that the international tax system has failed to keep up with the current economy. (22 words)
sentences.cn: In response to these many unilateral measures, the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) is currently working with 131 countries to reach a consensus by the end of 2020 on an international solution. (34 words)
sentences.words: Both France and the United States are involved in the organization’s work, but France’s digital services tax and the American response raise questions about what the future holds for the international tax system. (33 words)

sentences.id: France’s planned tax is a clear warning: Unless a broad consensus can be reached on reforming the international tax system, other nations are likely to follow suit, and American companies will face a cascade of different taxes from dozens of nations that will prove burdensome and costly. (47 words)
sentences.en:
2101
How can the train operators possibly justify yet another increase to rail passenger fares? (14 words)
sentences.cn: It has become a grimly reliable annual ritual: every January the cost of travelling by train rises, imposing a significant extra burden on those who have no option but to use the rail network to get to work or otherwise. (40 words)
sentences.words: This year’s rise, an average of 2.7 percent, may be a fraction lower than last year’s, but it is still well above the official Consumer Price Index (CPI) measure of inflation. (31 words)

sentences.id: Successive governments have permitted such increases on the grounds that the cost of investing in and running the rail network should be borne by those who use it, rather than the general taxpayer. (33 words)
sentences.en: Why, the argument goes, should a car-driving pensioner from Lincolnshire have to subsidise the daily commute of a stockbroker from Surrey? (21 words)
sentences.cn: Equally, there is a sense that the travails of commuters in the South East, many of whom will face among the biggest rises, have received too much attention compared to those who must endure the relatively poor infrastructure of the Midlands and the North. (44 words)
sentences.words: However, over the past 12 months, those commuters have also experienced some of the worst rail strikes in years. (19 words)

sentences.id: It is all very well train operators trumpeting the improvements they are making to the network, but passengers should be able to expect a basic level of service for the substantial sums they are now paying to travel. (38 words)
sentences.en: The responsibility for the latest wave of strikes rests on the unions. (12 words)
sentences.cn: However, there is a strong case that those who have been worst affected by industrial action should receive compensation for the disruption they have suffered. (25 words)
sentences.words: The Government has pledged to change the law to introduce a minimum service requirement so that, even when strikes occur, services can continue to operate. (25 words)

sentences.id: This should form part of a wider package of measures to address the long-running problems on Britain’s railways. (18 words)
sentences.en: Yes, more investment is needed, but passengers will not be willing to pay more indefinitely if they must also endure cramped, unreliable services, interrupted by regular chaos when timetables are changed, or planned maintenance is managed incompetently. (37 words)
sentences.cn: The threat of nationalisation may have been seen off for now, but it will return with a vengeance if the justified anger of passengers is not addressed in short order. (30 words)
sentences.words:
2102
 Last year marked the third year in a row that Indonesia’s bleak rate of deforestation has slowed in pace. (19 words)

sentences.id: One reason for the turnaround may be the country’s antipoverty program. (11 words)
sentences.en: In 2007, Indonesia started phasing in a program that gives money to its poorest residents under certain conditions, such as requiring people to keep kids in school or get regular medical care. (32 words)
sentences.cn: Called conditional cash transfers or CCTs, these social assistance programs are designed to reduce inequality and break the cycle of poverty. (21 words)
sentences.words: They’re already used in dozens of countries worldwide. (8 words)

sentences.id: In Indonesia, the program has provided enough food and medicine to substantially reduce severe growth problems among children. (18 words)
sentences.en: But CCT programs don’t generally consider effects on the environment. (10 words)
sentences.cn: In fact, poverty alleviation and environmental protection are often viewed as conflicting goals, says Paul Ferraro, an economist at Johns Hopkins University. (22 words)
sentences.words: That’s because economic growth can be correlated with environmental degradation, while protecting the environment is sometimes correlated with greater poverty. (20 words)

sentences.id: However, those correlations don’t prove cause and effect. (8 words)
sentences.en: The only previous study analyzing causality, based on an area in Mexico that had instituted CCTs, supported the traditional view. (20 words)
sentences.cn: There, as people got more money, some of them may have more cleared land for cattle to raise for meat, Ferraro says. (22 words)
sentences.words: Such programs do not have to negatively affect the environment, though. (11 words)

sentences.id: Ferraro wanted to see if Indonesia’s poverty-alleviation program was affecting deforestation. (11 words)
sentences.en: Indonesia has the third-largest area of tropical forest in the world and one of the highest deforestation rates. (18 words)
sentences.cn: Ferraro analyzed satellite data showing annual forest loss from 2008 to 2012—including during Indonesia’s phase-in of the antipoverty program—in 7,468 forested villages across 15 provinces. (27 words)
sentences.words: “We see that the program is associated with a 30 percent reduction in deforestation,” Ferraro says. (16 words)

sentences.id: That’s likely because the rural poor are using the money as makeshift insurance policies against inclement weather, Ferraro says. (19 words)
sentences.en: Typically, if rains are delayed, people may clear land to plant more rice to supplement their harvests. (17 words)
sentences.cn: With the CCTs, individuals instead can use the money to supplement their harvests. (13 words)
sentences.words: Whether this research translates elsewhere is anybody’s guess. (8 words)

sentences.id: Ferraro suggests the results may transfer to other parts of Asia, due to commonalities such as the importance of growing rice and market access. (24 words)
sentences.en: And regardless of transferability, the study shows that what’s good for people may also be good for the environment. (19 words)
sentences.cn:
Even if this program didn’t reduce poverty, Ferraro says, “the value of the avoided deforestation just for carbon dioxide emissions alone is more than the program costs.” (27 words)

2103
As a historian who’s always searching for the text or the image that makes us re-evaluate the past, I’ve become preoccupied with looking for photographs that show our Victorian ancestors smiling (what better way to shatter the image of 19th-century prudery?). (41 words)
sentences.words: I’ve found quite a few, and—since I started posting them on Twitter—they have been causing quite a stir. (20 words)

sentences.id: People have been surprised to see evidence that Victorians had fun and could, and did, laugh. (16 words)
sentences.en: They are noting that the Victorians suddenly seem to become more human as the hundred-or-so years that separate us fade away through our common experience of laughter. (27 words)
sentences.cn: Of course, I need to concede that my collection of “Smiling Victorians” makes up only a tiny percentage of the vast catalogue of photographic portraiture created between 1840 and 1900, the majority of which show sitters posing miserably and stiffly in front of painted backdrops, or staring absently into the middle distance. (52 words)
sentences.words: How do we explain this trend? (6 words)

sentences.id: During the 1840s and 1850s, in the early days of photography, exposure times were notoriously long: the daguerreotype photographic method (producing an image on a silvered copper plate) could take several minutes to complete, resulting in blurred images as sitters shifted position or adjusted their limbs. (46 words)
sentences.en: The thought of holding a fixed grin as the camera performed its magical duties was too much to contemplate, and so a non-committal blank stare became the norm. (28 words)
sentences.cn: But exposure times were much quicker by the 1880s, and the introduction of the Box Brownie and other portable cameras meant that, though slow by today’s digital standards, the exposure was almost instantaneous. (33 words)
sentences.words: Spontaneous smiles were relatively easy to capture by the 1890s, so we must look elsewhere for an explanation of why Victorians still hesitated to smile. (25 words)

sentences.id: One explanation might be the loss of dignity displayed through a cheesy grin. (13 words)
sentences.en: “Nature gave us lips to conceal our teeth,” ran one popular Victorian maxim, alluding to the fact that before the birth of proper dentistry, mouths were often in a shocking state of hygiene. (33 words)
sentences.cn: A flashing set of healthy and clean, regular “pearly whites” was a rare sight in Victorian society, the preserve of the super-rich (and even then, dental hygiene was not guaranteed). (30 words)
sentences.words: A toothy grin (especially when there were gaps or blackened teeth) lacked class: drunks, tramps and music hall performers might gurn and grin with a smile as wide as Lewis Carroll’s gum-exposing Cheshire Cat, but it was not a becoming look for properly bred persons. (45 words)

sentences.id: Even Mark Twain, a man who enjoyed a hearty laugh, said that when it came to photographic portraits there could be “nothing more damning than a silly, foolish smile fixed forever”. (31 words)
sentences.en:
2104
From the early days of broadband, advocates for consumers and web-based companies worried that the cable and phone companies selling broadband connections had the power and incentive to favor affiliated websites over their rivals’. (34 words)
sentences.cn: That’s why there has been such a strong demand for rules that would prevent broadband providers from picking winners and losers online, preserving the freedom and innovation that have been the lifeblood of the Internet. (35 words)
sentences.words: Yet that demand has been almost impossible to fill—in part because of pushback from broadband providers, anti-regulatory conservatives and the courts. (22 words)

sentences.id: A federal appeals court weighed in again Tuesday, but instead of providing a badly needed resolution, it only prolonged the fight. (21 words)
sentences.en: At issue before the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit was the latest take of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) on net neutrality, adopted on a party-line vote in 2017. (34 words)
sentences.cn: The Republican-penned order not only eliminated the strict net neutrality rules the FCC had adopted when it had a Democratic majority in 2015, but rejected the commission’s authority to require broadband providers to do much of anything. (37 words)
sentences.words: The order also declared that state and local governments couldn’t regulate broadband providers either. (14 words)

sentences.id: The commission argued that other agencies would protect against anti-competitive behavior, such as a broadband-providing conglomerate like AT&T favoring its own video-streaming service at the expense of Netflix and Apple TV. (31 words)
sentences.en: Yet the FCC also ended the investigations of broadband providers that imposed data caps on their rivals’ streaming services but not their own. (23 words)
sentences.cn: On Tuesday, the appeals court unanimously upheld the 2017 order deregulating broadband providers, citing a Supreme Court ruling from 2005 that upheld a similarly deregulatory move. (26 words)
sentences.words: But Judge Patricia Millett rightly argued in a concurring opinion that “the result is unhinged from the realities of modern broadband service,” and said Congress or the Supreme Court could intervene to “avoid trapping Internet regulation in technological anachronism.” (39 words)

sentences.id: In the meantime, the court threw out the FCC’s attempt to block all state rules on net neutrality, while preserving the commission’s power to preempt individual state laws that undermine its order. (32 words)
sentences.en: That means more battles like the one now going on between the Justice Department and California, which enacted a tough net neutrality law in the wake of the FCC’s abdication. (30 words)
sentences.cn: The endless legal battles and back-and-forth at the FCC cry out for Congress to act. (15 words)
sentences.words: It needs to give the commission explicit authority once and for all to bar broadband providers from meddling in the traffic on their network and to create clear rules protecting openness and innovation online. (34 words)

sentences.id:
2201
People often grumble that plastics are too durable. (8 words)
sentences.en: Water bottles, shopping bags, and other trash litter the planet, from Mount Everest to the Mariana Trench, because plastics are ubiquitous and don't break down easily. (26 words)
sentences.cn: But some plastic materials change over time. (7 words)
sentences.words: They crack and frizzle. (4 words)

sentences.id: They “weep” out additives. (4 words)
sentences.en: They melt into sludge. (4 words)
sentences.cn: All of which creates huge headaches for institutions, such as museums, trying to preserve culturally important objects. (17 words)
sentences.words: Until recently, museums only had to worry about traditional materials. (10 words)

sentences.id: The variety of plastic objects at risk is dizzying: early radios, avant-garde sculptures, celluloid animation stills from Disney films, David Bowie costumes, the first artificial heart. (26 words)
sentences.en: Certain artifacts are especially vulnerable because some pioneers in plastic art didn’t always know how to mix ingredients properly, says Thea van Oosten, a polymer chemist who, until retiring a few years ago, worked for decades at the Cultural Heritage Agency of the Netherlands (RCE). (45 words)
sentences.cn: “It’s like baking a cake: If you don’t have exact amounts, it goes wrong,” she says. (16 words)
sentences.words: “The object you make is already a time bomb.” (9 words)

sentences.id: And sometimes, it’s not the artist’s fault. (7 words)
sentences.en: In the 1960s, the Italian artist Piero Gilardi began to create hundreds of bright, colorful foam pieces. (17 words)
sentences.cn: Those pieces included small beds of roses and other items as well as a few dozen “nature carpet” — large rectangles decorated with foam pumpkins, cabbages, and watermelons. (27 words)
sentences.words: He wanted viewers to walk around on the carpets—which meant they had to be durable. (16 words)

sentences.id: Unfortunately, the polyurethane foam he used is inherently unstable. (9 words)
sentences.en: It’s especially vulnerable to light damage, and by the mid-1990s, Gilardi’s pumpkins, roses, and other figures were splitting and crumbling. (20 words)
sentences.cn: Museums locked some of them away in the dark. (9 words)
sentences.words: So van Oosten and colleagues at RCE began to study ways to protect polyurethane. (14 words)

sentences.id: First, they took foam samples similar to the nature carpets and infused some with stabilizing and consolidating chemicals that modern manufacturers often use. (23 words)
sentences.en: Van Oosten calls those chemicals “sunscreens” because their goal was to prevent further light damage and rebuild worn polymer fibers. (20 words)
sentences.cn: Then the team used xenon lamps to artificially age both treated and untreated samples, and examined them under high-powered microscopes. (20 words)
sentences.words: The results were encouraging. (4 words)

sentences.id: Samples that lacked sunscreen had withered under the barrage of photons: The molecular “struts” shoring up the foam were 42% thinner and notably more brittle than before the lamp treatment. (30 words)
sentences.en: The struts in samples with sunscreen decreased by as little as 12.5%. (12 words)
sentences.cn: Armed with that knowledge, conservators working with RCE infused several Gilardi sculptures, including two nature carpets, with the sunscreen to stabilize them. (22 words)
sentences.words: Van Oosten is proud that several have even gone on display again, albeit sometimes beneath protective cases. (17 words)

sentences.id: Long called the “queen of plastics,” in 2012, van Oosten was knighted in the Netherlands for her efforts to preserve plastic objects and spread knowledge to other institutes. (28 words)
sentences.en: Despite such success stories, preservation of plastics will likely get harder. (11 words)
sentences.cn: Old objects continue to deteriorate. (5 words)
sentences.words: Worse, biodegradable plastics, designed to disintegrate, are increasingly common. (9 words)

sentences.id: And more is at stake here than individual objects. (9 words)
sentences.en: Ferreira notes that archaeologists first defined the great material ages of human history — Stone Age, Iron Age, and so on — after examining artifacts in museums. (25 words)
sentences.cn:
We now live in an age of plastic, she says, “and what we decide to collect today, what we decide to preserve...will have a strong impact on how in the future we'll be seen.” (34 words)

2202
As the latest crop of students pen their UCAS form and weigh up their options, it may be   worth considering just how the point, purpose and value of a degree has changed and what Gen Z (and their parents) need to consider as they start the third stage of their educational journey. (53 words)
sentences.words: Millennials were told that if you did well in school, got a decent degree, you would be set up for life. (21 words)

sentences.id: But that promise has been found wanting. (7 words)
sentences.en: As degrees became universal, they became devalued (just as governments hiked up the cost of getting one). (17 words)
sentences.cn: Education was no longer a secure route of social mobility. (10 words)
sentences.words: Today, 28 per cent of graduates in the UK are in non-graduate roles; a percentage which is double the average amongst the OECD. (23 words)

sentences.id: This is not to say that there is no point in getting a degree, but, rather stress that a degree is not for everyone, that the switch from classroom to lecture hall is not an inevitable one and that other options are available. (43 words)
sentences.en: Thankfully, there are signs that this is already happening, with Gen Z seeking to learn from their millennial predecessors, even if parents and teachers tend to be still set in the degree mindset. (33 words)
sentences.cn: Employers have long seen the advantages of hiring school leavers who often prove themselves to be more committed and loyal employees than graduates. (23 words)
sentences.words: Many too are seeing the advantages of scrapping a degree requirement for certain roles—just as Penguin did in 2016. (20 words)

sentences.id: For those for whom a degree is the desired route, consider that this may well be the first of many. (20 words)
sentences.en: In this age of generalists, it pays to have specific knowledge or skills. (13 words)
sentences.cn: Postgraduates now earn 40 per cent more than graduates. (9 words)
sentences.words: When more and more of us have a degree, it makes sense (albeit expensive) to have two. (17 words)

sentences.id: It is unlikely that Gen Z will be done with education at 18 or 21; they will need to be constantly up-skilling throughout their career to stay agile, relevant and employable. (31 words)
sentences.en: It has been estimated that this generation due to the pressures of technology, the wish for personal fulfilment and desire for diversity will work for 17 different employers over the course of their working life and have five different careers. (40 words)
sentences.cn: Education, and not just knowledge gained on campus, will be a core part of Generation Z’s career trajectory. (18 words)
sentences.words: I have often heard older generations talk about their degree (even if it was gained decades ago) in the present and personal tense: “I am a geographer or ‘I am a classist’. (32 words)

sentences.id: Their sons or daughters would never say such a thing; it’s as if they already know that their degree won’t define them in the same way. (26 words)
sentences.en:
2203
Enlightening, stimulating, inspiring, fun. (4 words)
sentences.cn: These were some of the words that Nature readers used to describe their experiences of art-science collaborations in a series of articles on partnerships between artists and researchers. (28 words)
sentences.words: Nearly 40% of the roughly 350 people who responded to an accompanying poll said they had collaborated with artists; and almost all said they would consider doing so in future. (30 words)

sentences.id: Such an encouraging result is not surprising. (7 words)
sentences.en: Public engagement has become essential to many research projects. (9 words)
sentences.cn: Scientists are increasingly seeking out visual artists and designers to help them to communicate their work to new audiences. (19 words)
sentences.words: “Artists help scientists reach a broader audience and make emotional connections that enhance learning,” one respondent said. (17 words)

sentences.id: “The experience is very liberating for me, as a scientist,” said another. (12 words)
sentences.en: “There’s often a visual aspect to my science that generating and publishing data does not convey.” (16 words)
sentences.cn: One example of how artists and scientists have together rocked the senses came last month when the Sydney Symphony Orchestra in Australia performed a reworked version of Antonio Vivaldi’s The Four Seasons. (32 words)
sentences.words: They reimagined the 300-year-old score by injecting the latest climate prediction data for each season — provided by Monash University’s Climate Change Communication Research Hub in Melbourne. (26 words)

sentences.id: The work was entitled The (Uncertain) Four Seasons, and variations of the score containing local data were sent to every major orchestra in the world. (25 words)
sentences.en: The performance was a creative call to action ahead of November’s United Nations. (13 words)
sentences.cn: But a genuine partnership must be a two-way street. (9 words)
sentences.words: Fewer artists than scientists responded to the Nature poll; however, several respondents noted that artists do not simply assist scientists with their communication requirements. (24 words)

sentences.id: Nor should their work be considered only as an object of study —even if these are reasons why scientists seek opportunities to work with artists. (25 words)
sentences.en: The alliances are most valuable when scientists and artists have a shared stake in a project, are able to jointly design it and can critique each other’s work. (28 words)
sentences.cn: Such an approach can both prompt new research as well as result in powerful art. (15 words)
sentences.words: More than half a century ago, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) opened its Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) in Cambridge to explore the role of technology in culture. (30 words)

sentences.id: The centre was established during the Vietnam War, when many scientists in the United States were being criticized for working on defence contracts. (23 words)
sentences.en: Its founders believed that artists and scientists could, together, create a vision for a more humane world. (17 words)
sentences.cn: They deliberately focused their projects around light — hence the ‘visual studies’ in the name. (14 words)
sentences.words: Light was a something that both artists and scientists had an interest in, and therefore could form the basis of collaboration, says Seth Riskin, a visual-arts researcher at the MIT Museum who previously worked at CAVS. (36 words)

sentences.id: The reach of art-science tie-ups needs to go beyond the necessary purpose of research communication, and participants must not fall into the trap of stereotyping each other. (27 words)
sentences.en: Artists and scientists alike are immersed in discovery and invention, and challenge and critique are core to both, too. (19 words)
sentences.cn:
2204
The personal grievance provisions of the Employment Relations Act 2000 (ERA) prevent  
an employer from firing an employee without good cause. (22 words)
sentences.words: Instead, dismissals must be justified. (5 words)

sentences.id: Employers must both show cause and act in a procedurally fair way. (12 words)
sentences.en: Personal grievance procedures were designed to guard the jobs of ordinary workers from “unjustified dismissals”. (15 words)
sentences.cn: The premise was that the common law of contract lacked sufficient safeguards for workers against arbitrary conduct by management. (19 words)
sentences.words: Long gone are the days when a boss could simply give an employee contractual notice. (15 words)

sentences.id: But these provisions create difficulties for businesses when applied to highly paid managers and executives. (15 words)
sentences.en: As countless boards and business owners will attest, constraining firms from firing poorly performing, high-earning managers is a handbrake on boosting productivity and overall performance. (25 words)
sentences.cn: The difference between C-grade and A-grade managers may very well be the difference between business success or failure. (18 words)
sentences.words: Between preserving the jobs of ordinary workers or losing them. (10 words)

sentences.id: Yet mediocrity is no longer enough to justify a dismissal. (10 words)
sentences.en: Consequently — and paradoxically — laws introduced to protect the jobs of ordinary workers may be placing those jobs at risk. (19 words)
sentences.cn: If not placing jobs at risk, to the extent employment protection laws constrain business owners from dismissing underperforming managers, those laws act as a constraint on firm productivity and therefore on workers’ wages. (33 words)
sentences.words: Indeed, in “An International Perspective on New Zealand’s Productivity Paradox” (2014), the Productivity Commission singled out the low quality of managerial capabilities as a cause of the country’s poor productivity growth record. (32 words)

sentences.id: Nor are highly paid managers themselves immune from the harm caused by the ERA’s unjustified dismissal procedures. (17 words)
sentences.en: Because employment protection laws make it costlier to fire an employee, employers are more cautious about hiring new staff. (19 words)
sentences.cn: This makes it harder for the marginal manager to gain employment. (11 words)
sentences.words: And firms pay staff less because firms carry the burden of the employment arrangement going wrong. (16 words)

sentences.id: Society also suffers from excessive employment protections. (7 words)
sentences.en: Stringent job dismissal regulations adversely affect productivity growth and hamper both prosperity and overall wellbeing. (15 words)
sentences.cn: Across the Tasman, Australia deals with the unjustified dismissal paradox by excluding employees earning above a specified “high-income threshold” from the protection of its unfair dismissal laws. (27 words)
sentences.words: In New Zealand, a 2016 private members’ Bill tried to permit firms and high-income employees to contract out of the unjustified dismissal regime. (23 words)

sentences.id: However, the mechanisms proposed were unwieldy and the Bill was voted down following the change in government later that year. (20 words)
sentences.en:
2301
The weather in Texas may have cooled since the recent extreme heat, but the temperature will be high at the State Board of Education meeting in Austin this month as officials debate how climate change is taught in Texas schools. (40 words)
sentences.cn: Pat Hardy, who sympathises with the views of the energy sector, is resisting proposed changes to science standards for pre-teen pupils. (21 words)
sentences.words: “There are as many scientists working against all the panic of global climate change as there are those who are pushing it,” she claims. (24 words)

sentences.id: “Texas is an energy state and we need to recognise that. (11 words)
sentences.en: You need to remember where your bread is buttered.”(9 words)
sentences.cn: Most scientists and experts sharply dispute Hardy’s views. (8 words)
sentences.words: Board members like her “casually dismiss the career work of scholars and scientists as just another misguided opinion,” says Dan Quinn, senior communications strategist at the Texas Freedom Network, a non-profit group that monitors public education. (36 words)

sentences.id: Such debates reflect fierce discussions across the US, as researchers, policymakers, teachers and students step up demands for a greater focus on teaching about the facts of climate change in schools. (31 words)
sentences.en: A study last year by the National Center for Science Education, a non-profit group of scientists and teachers, looking at how state public schools across the country address climate change in science classes, gave barely half of US states a grade B+ or higher. (44 words)
sentences.cn: Among the 10 worst performers were some of the most populous states, including Texas, which was given the lowest grade (F) and has a disproportionate influence because its textbooks are widely sold elsewhere. (33 words)
sentences.words: Glenn Branch, the centre’s deputy director, cautions that setting state-level science standards is only one limited benchmark in a country that decentralises decisions to local school boards. (27 words)

sentences.id: Even if a state is considered a high performer in its science standards, “that does not mean it will be taught”, he says. (23 words)
sentences.en: Another issue is that, while climate change is well integrated into some subjects and at some ages—such as earth and space sciences in high schools—it is not as well represented in curricula for younger children and in subjects that are more widely taught, such as biology and chemistry. (50 words)
sentences.cn: It is also less prominent in many social studies courses. (10 words)
sentences.words: Branch points out that, even if a growing number of official guidelines and textbooks reflect scientific consensus on climate change, unofficial educational materials that convey more biased perspectives are being distributed to teachers. (33 words)

sentences.id: They include materials sponsored by energy industry associations. (8 words)
sentences.en:
2302
Communities throughout New England have been attempting to regulate short-term rentals since sites like Airbnb took off in the 2010s. (20 words)
sentences.cn: Now, with record-high home prices and historically low inventory, there’s an increased urgency in such regulation, particularly among those who worry that developers will come in and buy up swaths of housing to flip for a fortune on the short-term rental market. (42 words)
sentences.words: In New Hampshire, where the rental vacancy rate has dropped below 1 percent, housing advocates fear unchecked short-term rentals will put further pressure on an already strained market. (28 words)

sentences.id: The state Legislature recently voted against a bill that would’ve made it illegal for towns to create legislation restricting short-term rentals. (21 words)
sentences.en: “We are at a crisis level on the supply of rental housing,” said Nick Taylor, executive director of the Workforce Housing Coalition of the Greater Seacoast. (26 words)
sentences.cn: Without enough affordable housing in southern New Hampshire towns, “employers are having a hard time attracting employees, and workers are having a hard time finding a place to live,” Taylor said. (31 words)
sentences.words: However, short-term rentals also provide housing for tourists, pointed out Ryan Castle, CEO of a local association of realtors. (19 words)

sentences.id: “A lot of workers are servicing the tourist industry, and the tourism industry is serviced by those people coming in short term,” Castle said, “and so it’s a cyclical effect.” (30 words)
sentences.en: Short-term rentals themselves are not the crux of the issue, said Keren Horn, an expert on affordable housing policy. (19 words)
sentences.cn: “I think individuals being able to rent out their second home is a good thing. (15 words)
sentences.words: If it’s their vacation home anyway, and it’s just empty, why can’t you make money off it?” Horn said. (19 words)

sentences.id: Issues arise, however, when developers attempt to create large-scale short-term rental facilities—de facto hotels—to bypass taxes and regulations. (20 words)
sentences.en: “I think the question is, shouldn’t a developer who’s really building a hotel, but disguising it as not a hotel, be treated and taxed and regulated like a hotel?” Horn said. (31 words)
sentences.cn: At the end of 2018, Governor Charlie Baker of Massachusetts signed a bill to rein in those potential investor-buyers. (19 words)
sentences.words: The bill requires every rental host to register with the state, mandates they carry insurance, and opens the potential for local taxes on top of a new state levy. (29 words)

sentences.id: Boston took things even further, requiring renters to register with the city’s Inspectional Services Department. (15 words)
sentences.en:
Horn said similar registration requirements could benefit struggling cities and towns, but “if we want to make a change in the housing market, the main one is we have to build a lot more.” (34 words)

2303
If you’re heading for your nearest branch of Waterstones, the biggest book retailer in the UK, in search of the Duchess of Sussex’s new children’s book The Bench, you might have to be prepared to hunt around a bit; the same may be true of The President’s Daughter, the new thriller by Bill Clinton and James Patterson. (57 words)
sentences.cn: Both of these books are published next week by Penguin Random House (PRH), a company currently involved in a stand-off with Waterstones. (22 words)
sentences.words: The problem began late last year, when PRH confirmed that it had introduced a credit limit with Waterstones “at a very significant level”. (23 words)

sentences.id: The trade magazine The Bookseller reported that Waterstones branch managers were being told to remove PRH books from prominent areas such as tables, display spaces and windows, and were “quietly retiring them to their relevant sections”. (36 words)
sentences.en: PRH declined to comment on the issue, but a spokesperson for Waterstones told me: “Waterstones are currently operating with reduced credit terms from PRH, the only publisher in the UK to place any limitations on our ability to trade. (39 words)
sentences.cn: We are not boycotting PRH titles but we are doing our utmost to ensure that availability for customers remains good despite the lower overall levels of stock. (27 words)
sentences.words: We are hopeful with our shops now open again that normality will return and that we will be allowed to buy appropriately. (22 words)

sentences.id: Certainly, our shops are exceptionally busy. (6 words)
sentences.en: The sales for our May Books of the Month surpassed any month since 2018.” (14 words)
sentences.cn: In the meantime, PRH authors have been the losers. (9 words)
sentences.words: Big-name PRH authors may suffer a bit, but it’s those mid-list authors, who normally rely on Waterstones staff’s passion for promoting books by lesser-known writers, who will be praying for an end to the dispute. (35 words)

sentences.id: It comes at a time when authors are already worried about the consequences of the proposed merger between PRH and another big publisher, Simon & Schuster—the reduction in the number of unaligned UK publishers is likely to lead to fewer bidding wars, lower advances, and more conformity in terms of what is published. (54 words)
sentences.en: “This is all part of a wider change towards concentration of power,” says literary agent Andrew Lownie. (17 words)
sentences.cn: “The publishing industry talks about diversity in terms of authors and staff but it also needs a plurality of ways of delivering intellectual contact, choice and different voices. (28 words)
sentences.words: After all, many of the most interesting books in recent years have come from small publishers.” (16 words)

sentences.id: We shall see whether that plurality is a casualty of the current need among publishers to be big enough to take on all-comers. (23 words)
sentences.en:
2304
Scientific papers are the recordkeepers of progress in research. (9 words)
sentences.cn: Each year researchers publish millions of papers in more than 30,000 journals. (12 words)
sentences.words: The scientific community measures the quality of those papers in a number of ways, including the perceived quality of the journal (as reflected by the title’s impact factor) and the number of citations a specific paper accumulates. (37 words)

sentences.id: The careers of scientists and the reputation of their institutions depend on the number and prestige of the papers they produce, but even more so on the citations attracted by these papers. (32 words)
sentences.en: Citation cartels, where journals, authors, and institutions conspire to inflate citation numbers, have existed for a long time. (18 words)
sentences.cn: In 2016, researchers developed an algorithm to recognize suspicious citation patterns, including groups of authors that disproportionately cite one another and groups of journals that cite each other frequently to increase the impact factors of their publications. (37 words)
sentences.words: Recently, another expression of this predatory behavior has emerged: so-called support service consultancies that provide language and other editorial support to individual authors and to journals sometimes advise contributors to add a number of citations to their articles. (38 words)

sentences.id: The advent of electronic publishing and authors’ need to find outlets for their papers resulted in thousands of new journals. (20 words)
sentences.en: The birth of predatory journals wasn’t far behind. (8 words)
sentences.cn: These journals can act as milk cows where every single article in an issue may cite a specific paper or a series of papers. (24 words)
sentences.words: In some instances, there is absolutely no relationship between the content of the article and the citations. (17 words)

sentences.id: The peculiar part is that the journal that the editor is supposedly working for is not profiting at all—it is just providing citations to other journals. (27 words)
sentences.en: Such practices can lead an article to accrue more than 150 citations in the same year that it was published. (20 words)
sentences.cn: How insidious is this type of citation manipulation? (8 words)
sentences.words: In one example, an individual—acting as author, editor, and consultant—was able to use at least 15 journals as citation providers to articles published by five scientists at three universities. (31 words)

sentences.id: The problem is rampant in Scopus, a citation database, which includes a high number of the new “international” journals. (19 words)
sentences.en: In fact, a listing in Scopus seems to be a criterion to be targeted in this type of citation manipulation. (20 words)
sentences.cn: Scopus itself has all the data necessary to detect this malpractice. (11 words)
sentences.words: Red flags include a large number of citations to an article within the first year. (15 words)
